_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: As of June 2026, WTI crude is priced around $79.2 per barrel, Brent crude at $81.62 per barrel, and regular US gasoline at $3.85 per gallon. Henry Hub natural gas stands at $2.83 per MMBtu. Natural gas has been the most volatile fuel among these three, as shown by an index comparison to 2019 (indexed at 100), with natural gas prices spiking the hardest in 2022 before retreating, whereas crude and gasoline prices have moved more closely in tandem. This volatility reflects natural gas's sensitivity to supply-demand fluctuations and geopolitical factors.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity (LCOE), which measures the average cost to produce electricity from a generating asset over its lifetime per megawatt-hour, illustrates significant shifts in energy economics. Currently, solar PV has a LCOE of $44/MWh and onshore wind $33/MWh, both of which undercut the cost of new coal and gas plants, at $110/MWh and $76/MWh respectively. Offshore wind stands at $75/MWh, slightly below gas and well below coal. Since 2010, solar PV costs have fallen by about 90% from roughly $460/MWh to $44/MWh, demonstrating rapid technological progress and cost reduction in renewable energy.
notes.currency: The estimated share of global oil trade settled outside the US dollar is approximately 20% and increasing, though there is no official figure for this metric. Despite this gradual shift, the US dollar still dominates oil transactions worldwide. Key drivers of this trend include Russia's strategic pivot to Asian buyers and increased oil cargoes settled in yuan and rupees, contributing to diversification in currency usage for oil trade.
notes.now: Currently, fossil fuel prices reflect both underlying market conditions and historic volatility patterns, particularly in natural gas. Meanwhile, the transition to renewable energy is underpinned by sharply declining levelised costs for solar PV and onshore wind, making them cost-competitive or cheaper than new fossil fuel generation. Concurrently, global oil trade exhibits a moderate shift away from the US dollar, influenced by geopolitical realignments, yet the dollar remains predominant. Together, these factors shape a complex but measured energy landscape.
